name: Debug Playbook Failures with Verbosity and Check Mode
description: Diagnose and fix Ansible playbook failures using increased verbosity levels, debug module, check mode, and step-by-step execution.

steps.title: Run with increased verbosity
steps.description: Shows SSH connection details, module arguments, and full return values. Use -vvvv for connection-level debugging.
steps.tip: Start with -v and increase verbosity only if you need more detail. -vvvv shows raw SSH commands.

steps.title: Use the debug module
steps.description: Add debug tasks in your playbook to print variable values: 'debug: var=my_variable' or 'debug: msg="Value is {{ my_var }}"'.

steps.title: Run in step mode
steps.description: Prompts you before each task with yes/no/continue, letting you step through the playbook interactively.

steps.title: Start at a specific task
steps.description: Skips all tasks before the named task. Useful when you know which task is failing.

steps.title: Check for syntax errors
steps.description: Validates YAML syntax and checks for common mistakes and best practice violations.

fullScript:
# Ansible troubleshooting workflow
ansible-playbook site.yml --syntax-check
ansible-lint site.yml
ansible-playbook site.yml --check --diff
ansible-playbook site.yml -vvv
ansible-playbook site.yml --step
ansible-playbook site.yml --start-at-task "Deploy nginx config"

faq.question: My task says 'changed' but nothing happened. Why?
faq.answer: The module reported a change but it may not have had the expected effect. Use -v to see the full module return values. Check that you are targeting the right hosts and using the correct module parameters.

faq.question: How do I see which hosts failed after a playbook run?
faq.answer: Ansible prints a PLAY RECAP at the end showing ok, changed, unreachable, and failed counts per host. You can also use the 'json' callback plugin for machine-readable output.

faq.question: How do I ignore errors on a specific task?
faq.answer: Add 'ignore_errors: true' to the task. For more control, use 'failed_when: false' or register the result and check it conditionally with 'failed_when: result.rc != 0 and result.rc != 2'.
